---
title: |
  Machine Learning for Characterization and Processing<br>Unit 11: Explainability and Uncertainty in Lab Decisions
bibliography: ref.bib
author:
  - name: Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz
    affiliation:
      - FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg
execute:
  eval: false
format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    css: custom.css
    theme: custom.scss
    slide-number: c/t
    logo: "eclipse_logo_small.png"
    footer: "© Philipp Pelz - ML for Characterization and Processing"
---

## 01. Title: Explainability and Uncertainty in Lab Decisions
- Unit role and scope.

## 02. Learning outcomes
- Build decision workflows with confidence awareness.

## 03. Recap from Unit 10
- Real-time deployment constraints.

## 04. Why explainability in experiments
- Trust and accountability in lab actions.

## 05. Why uncertainty matters operationally
- Abstain/escalate vs auto-act.

## 06. Global vs local explanations
- Different user needs.

## 07. Intrinsic vs post-hoc explanations
- Tradeoffs in model choice.

## 08. Saliency methods overview
- Heatmap-style interpretations.

## 09. Attribution pitfalls
- Sensitivity and instability.

## 10. Feature importance methods
- Permutation/SHAP overview.

## 11. Counterfactual explanations
- Actionable what-if guidance.

## 12. Example-based explanations
- Prototypes and nearest neighbors.

## 13. Aleatoric vs epistemic uncertainty
- Source decomposition.

## 14. Predictive intervals and confidence maps
- Spatial/temporal uncertainty outputs.

## 15. Calibration diagnostics
- Reliability diagrams and ECE.

## 16. Miscalibration in lab ML
- Overconfident failure cases.

## 17. OOD detection in instruments
- Shift and novelty alerts.

## 18. Uncertainty-triggered control policies
- Human review thresholds.

## 19. Decision-theoretic framing
- Utility and risk tradeoffs.

## 20. Combining explanation + uncertainty
- Better triage decisions.

## 21. Interface design for operators
- Presenting confidence effectively.

## 22. Cognitive overload risks
- Keep explanations actionable.

## 23. Time-budgeted explainability
- Real-time vs offline analysis.

## 24. Validation protocol
- Human-grounded and task-grounded tests.

## 25. Robustness tests
- Perturbation and stress scenarios.

## 26. Failure mode #1
- Plausible but wrong explanations.

## 27. Failure mode #2
- Low uncertainty on OOD samples.

## 28. Failure mode #3
- Operator overtrust automation.

## 29. Mitigation checklist
- Governance and guardrails.

## 30. Logging and audit trails
- Traceable decision histories.

## 31. Regulatory/quality context
- Explainability documentation requirements.

## 32. Human-in-the-loop workflows
- Override and escalation design.

## 33. Case sketch: microscopy defect triage
- End-to-end scaffold.

## 34. Case sketch: spectroscopy-based process alarm
- End-to-end scaffold.

## 35. Case sketch: autonomous experiment gating
- End-to-end scaffold.

## 36. Visualization patterns
- Confidence overlays and rationale cards.

## 37. Reporting standards
- Include uncertainty + explanation metrics.

## 38. Reproducibility requirements
- Fixed eval suites and seeds.

## 39. Bridge to Unit 12
- Closed-loop experiment control.

## 40. Exercise scaffold: calibration + explanation audit
- Joint reliability check.

## 41. Exercise scaffold: threshold design
- Define abstain/escalate policy.

## 42. Exercise scaffold: OOD challenge set
- Evaluate detection robustness.

## 43. Exercise scaffold: interface mockup
- Communicate model confidence.

## 44. Exercise scaffold: incident post-mortem
- Analyze a simulated failure.

## 45. Exam-oriented key statements
- High-yield concepts.

## 46. Summary table
- Methods, benefits, limitations.

## 47. Unit summary
- Core takeaways.

## 48. Reading assignment
- Unit 12 control-loop preparation.

## 49. References
- Source list.

## 50. Q&A buffer slide
- Discussion and synthesis.

::: {#refs}
:::
